title: Staff Lifecycle: Recruitment, Induction, Appraisal, PD, Wellbeing

content.text: The quality of education in any school is fundamentally determined by the quality of its teachers and staff. At Eduversal Partner Schools, we recognize that attracting, developing, and retaining exceptional educators is central to achieving our mission: delivering world-class Cambridge International education while upholding Indonesian values and preparing students for global success. This section provides a comprehensive framework for managing the complete staff lifecycle across all Eduversal Partner Schools in Indonesia. It addresses the unique context of our schools: delivering Cambridge Primary, Lower Secondary, IGCSE, and AS & A Level programmes alongside Indonesian national curriculum subjects (Pendidikan Pancasila, Bahasa Indonesia, Religion, and Civics), serving diverse student populations (English as Additional Language learners), and operating in varied settings including urban and rural locations, day schools, boarding schools, and Islamic boarding schools.
Effective staff lifecycle management serves several critical purposes:

items: Quality Assurance: Ensuring all staff members possess the qualifications, competencies, and professional dispositions required to deliver high-quality Cambridge education and meet Indonesian regulatory standards.
Student Outcomes: Directly improving student learning, wellbeing, and achievement through effective teaching, pastoral care, and support services.
Continuous Improvement: Fostering a professional culture where staff are supported, developed, and empowered to grow throughout their careers.
Compliance and Accreditation: Meeting Indonesian employment law requirements, Cambridge Centre standards, and CIS/WASC accreditation criteria for professional staffing.
Retention and Morale: Creating positive work environments where talented educators choose to build their careers and contribute to school improvement over the long term.
content.title: Purpose of Comprehensive Staff Lifecycle Management

content.text: Research consistently demonstrates that teacher quality is the single most significant school-based factor influencing student achievement. At Eduversal Partner Schools, we observe this directly through our Quality Management System (QMS) data, which reveals strong correlations between teacher appraisal ratings, professional development engagement, and student performance on internal assessments (EASE) and Cambridge examinations. Effective staff members contribute to student success by:

items: Delivering engaging, well-planned lessons that promote deep learning and critical thinking
Providing differentiated support for diverse learners, including EAL students and those with special educational needs
Creating safe, inclusive, and positive classroom environments where all students can thrive
Building strong relationships with students that support their academic, social, and emotional development
Using assessment data strategically to inform instruction and intervention
Modeling the Eduversal Student Learning Outcomes (SLOs) and Profil Pelajar Pancasila competencies in their professional practice
content.title: Link Between Staff Quality and Student Outcomes

content.text: Staff lifecycle management is directly aligned with the Eduversal mission: to provide exceptional international education that develops globally competent, ethically grounded graduates who excel academically and contribute meaningfully to society. Every element of recruitment, induction, appraisal, and professional development is designed to ensure staff embody and advance our six Student Learning Outcomes:

steps: Academic Excellence: Staff possess deep subject knowledge and pedagogical expertise to prepare students for Cambridge qualifications and Indonesian national assessments.
Communication: Staff model effective multilingual communication (English and Bahasa Indonesia) and support students' language development across all subjects.
Global Competence: Staff bring international perspectives, celebrate cultural diversity, and prepare students to engage with global issues.
Ethical Leadership: Staff demonstrate integrity, professionalism, and ethical decision-making, serving as role models for students.
Innovation and Creativity: Staff employ innovative teaching strategies, integrate technology effectively, and encourage creative problem-solving.
Wellbeing: Staff prioritize student mental health, safety, and holistic development, creating supportive learning environments.
content.title: Alignment with School Mission and Student Learning Outcomes

content.text: Eduversal Partner Schools are committed to fostering a professional culture characterized by continuous improvement, collaboration, and mutual support. This culture is built on several foundational principles:

items: Growth Mindset: We believe all staff can develop their practice through focused effort, quality feedback, and ongoing learning.
Reflective Practice: Teachers regularly reflect on their teaching, analyze student data, and make evidence-informed adjustments to improve outcomes.
Collaborative Learning: Professional Learning Communities (PLCs), peer observations, and mentoring enable staff to learn from one another and share effective practices.
Evidence-Based Decision Making: Professional development and improvement efforts are guided by data from the Appraisal Program, EASE assessments, Cambridge exam results, and school-wide KPIs.

Developmental Approach to Evaluation: Our appraisal system emphasizes growth and support rather than punitive measures, recognizing that effective teachers develop over time with high-quality feedback and targeted professional development.
content.title: Professional Culture and Continuous Improvement Mindset

content.text: The Development of Teaching Proficiency (DTP) programme and Appraisal Program, delivered through Eduversal's Educamp platform, are central to this continuous improvement culture. Through regular observations, coaching, workshops, and collaborative planning, we ensure every staff member has opportunities to strengthen their practice and advance their careers. The following sections provide detailed guidance on each phase of the staff lifecycle, from recruitment through to exit, enabling Principals and HR teams to build and sustain exceptional educational teams.

Strategic workforce planning ensures that schools have the right number of qualified staff in the right roles at the right time. This proactive approach supports continuity, quality, and fiscal responsibility across Eduversal Partner Schools.
Each school conducts a comprehensive staffing review at least annually (typically in Term 3 for the following academic year) that includes:

items: Enrollment Projections: Analysis of current enrollment, projected student numbers by year group, and anticipated growth or decline. Data sources include application trends, student retention rates, and demographic patterns in the school's catchment area.

Class Size Planning: Determination of optimal class sizes aligned with Cambridge recommendations (typically 20-24 students for Primary, 20-24 for Lower Secondary, 18-20 for IGCSE practical subjects, 14-18 for AS & A Level) and Indonesian regulations (maximum 28 for Primary, 32 for Secondary).

Curriculum Requirements: Assessment of teaching loads across all subjects including Cambridge international subjects, Indonesian national curriculum subjects (Pancasila, Bahasa Indonesia, Religion, Civics), and specialist areas (EAL support, SEN, library, counseling, extracurricular coordination).

Subject Expertise Gaps: Identification of subjects where additional expertise is needed, particularly for specialized Cambridge qualifications (e.g., Further Mathematics, Computer Science, specific language options, Design & Technology).
Support Staff Needs: Review of administrative, technical, boarding, and facility staff requirements to ensure smooth operations.
content.title: Annual Staffing Review Process

content.text: Eduversal Partner Schools maintain clear qualification standards for all staff positions. The following matrix provides minimum requirements; schools may set higher standards based on local context:

rows: Bachelor's degree in relevant subject or education Teaching qualification (Postgraduate Certificate in Education (PGCE), Bachelor of Education (B.Ed.), teaching license, or equivalent) Cambridge training or experience (preferred; induction provided) Minimum 2 years teaching experience for standard roles; 5+ years for senior roles Police clearance from country of origin

S1 (Bachelor's) degree in relevant subject or education Sertifikat Pendidik (teaching certification) or working toward certification Proficiency in subject area (Pancasila, Bahasa Indonesia, Religion, or Civics) SKCK (Surat Keterangan Catatan Kepolisian) police clearance

Bachelor's degree minimum; Master's preferred Teaching qualification \+ Cambridge training Minimum 5 years teaching experience, including 2+ years at relevant level Demonstrated instructional leadership and curriculum development skills Experience with teacher mentoring and professional development

Bachelor's degree in Psychology, Counseling, or Social Work Master's degree in School Counseling or Clinical Psychology (preferred) Professional counseling certification or license Experience working with children and adolescents Knowledge of developmental psychology and mental health issues

Bachelor's degree in Education, Linguistics, or related field TESOL, TEFL, CELTA, or equivalent EAL teaching qualification Experience teaching EAL learners in international school settings Knowledge of CEFR framework and language acquisition theory

Bachelor's degree in Special Education or Education Qualification in learning support, special needs education, or educational psychology Experience with diverse learning needs (dyslexia, ADHD, autism spectrum, etc.) Familiarity with Cambridge access arrangements and IEP development

Bachelor's degree; degree or certificate in Library Science preferred Experience with library management systems and digital resources Knowledge of age-appropriate literature and research skills instruction
Relevant technical diploma or degree in subject area Technical expertise in equipment operation and maintenance Understanding of health and safety protocols (particularly for science labs)
content.title: Qualification Matrix for Different Roles

content.text: Note: The General Manager (GM) or Foundation Representative oversees compliance with these standards across the Eduversal network. Schools experiencing difficulty recruiting staff who meet minimum qualifications should consult with the GM before making offers.
Eduversal Partner Schools aim to maintain staff-student ratios that balance educational quality with operational sustainability:

items: Overall School Ratio: Target 1:10 (including all academic staff and leadership)
Early Years: 1:8-10 (teacher-student) with classroom assistants
Primary (Years 1-6): 1:10-12 average class size; specialist teachers for PE, Music, Art
Lower Secondary (Years 7-9): 1:20-24 average class size; subject specialist teachers
IGCSE (Years 10-11): 1:18-24 depending on subject; smaller for practical subjects (Science, Design, ICT)
AS & A Level (Years 12-13): 1:8-16 to enable individualized support and university preparation
Support Staff: EAL support (1:40-60 EAL learners), SEN support (1:30-50 students), Counselors (1:200-300 students), Librarian (1 per school minimum)
content.title: Staff-Student Ratios

content.text: These ratios serve as guidelines; actual staffing decisions consider school size, student needs, curriculum offerings, and budget constraints. Schools with significant EAL or SEN populations may require additional support staff.

International schools typically experience higher staff turnover than local schools due to the mobile nature of expatriate teachers. Effective succession planning minimizes disruption and maintains quality:

items: Annual Staff Intentions Survey: In Term 3, all staff complete a confidential survey indicating their intentions for the following year. This provides early warning for recruitment needs.
Notice Requirements: Employment contracts specify minimum notice periods (typically 90 days before end of academic year or 30 days mid-year with penalties). See Section 12.4 for contract details.
Leadership Pipeline Development: Identify and develop emerging leaders through Subject Leader shadowing, professional development opportunities, and acting roles during leave periods.
Knowledge Transfer: Departing staff complete handover documentation including curriculum plans, student information, and resource inventories. Exit procedures are outlined in Section 12.12.

Rotational Staff Pool: Eduversal maintains a network-wide pool of rotational staff (teachers and managers) who can be deployed to partner schools based on needs. The GM coordinates rotational assignments; see Juknis partnership agreements for rotation policies.
content.title: Anticipating Turnover and Succession Planning

content.text: Staffing is typically the largest operating expense (60-75% of school budgets). Workforce planning must balance educational priorities with financial sustainability:

steps: Annual Staffing Budget Preparation: The Principal, in collaboration with the GM and Finance team, prepares a detailed staffing budget for the following academic year, typically due in Term 2. This budget includes:
content.title: Budget Considerations and Yayasan Approval

items: Salary and benefits for all positions (base salary, housing allowance, transportation, health insurance, pension/BPJS, professional development)
Recruitment costs (advertising, interviews, relocation support for expatriate teachers)
Work permits and visa costs for expatriate teachers (KITAS/IMTA processing; see Section 2.3 for Indonesian compliance)
Substitute teacher provisions

steps: Yayasan Review and Approval: The proposed staffing budget is submitted to the Yayasan Board for review and approval. The GM supports this process by providing context on network-wide staffing patterns and Eduversal quality standards.

Mid-Year Adjustments: Unexpected staffing changes mid-year (resignations, enrollment shifts) may require budget adjustments. The Principal consults with the GM and Yayasan before creating new positions or eliminating existing ones.

Salary Scales and Competitiveness: Salaries are benchmarked against comparable international schools in Indonesia to attract and retain quality staff while respecting local economic conditions. The GM provides guidance on competitive compensation packages.

content.text: Example: A mid-sized Eduversal school (500 students, Primary through A Level) might employ 40-50 teaching staff, 8-12 support staff (counselors, librarian, EAL/SEN specialists, admin), 3-5 lab/technical staff, and a 4-6 person leadership team (Principal, Vice Principal, Subject Leaders). The staffing budget would include provisions for at least 2-3 replacement teachers annually to account for normal turnover.
Where to Recruit: International Channels (for Cambridge subject teachers):

steps: International teacher recruitment websites: Search Associates, ISS-Schrole, TES, TIE Online
University career services (education faculties in UK, Australia, NZ, Canada, USA)
Professional networks: Cambridge International Education LinkedIn groups, IB/international school Facebook groups
Direct school website careers page with attractive employer branding
content.title: Recruitment Strategy

content.text: Local Channels (for Indonesian national subject teachers and support staff):

steps: Indonesian university career centers (UPI Bandung, UNJ Jakarta, UNY Yogyakarta, UNNES Semarang)
Professional teacher associations and Islamic education networks (for Islamic boarding schools)
Local job portals: JobStreet Indonesia, Kalibrr, LinkedIn Indonesia
Referrals from current staff and Eduversal network schools
Internal job postings across partner schools enable staff mobility
Rotational staff program managed by GM for high-performing teachers
Alumni network of former staff who may return
Clear job title and grade level (e.g., "Primary Year 3 Homeroom Teacher" or "IGCSE Biology Teacher")
Comprehensive job description including teaching load, subjects, pastoral responsibilities
Required and preferred qualifications (degree, teaching license, Cambridge training, experience)
Realistic expectations about school context (EAL learners, boarding school, Islamic ethos, rural/urban setting)
Compensation package overview (salary range, housing allowance, health insurance, visa sponsorship for expatriates, professional development opportunities)
School profile and location with photos/videos showing facilities, student life, and local community
Student Learning Outcomes and values to attract mission-aligned candidates
Application process and timeline with clear instructions and deadlines

content.text: Example Advertisement: "Islamic International School seeks experienced Cambridge IGCSE Mathematics Teacher for August 2026 start. Join our growing school of 400 students in Central Java, delivering Cambridge curriculum alongside Indonesian national subjects. Ideal candidate: Bachelor's in Mathematics, teaching qualification, 3+ years secondary experience, comfortable with EAL learners. Competitive salary, housing allowance, health insurance, visa support, ongoing Cambridge PD. Islamic ethos, boarding community. Apply by March 15." Employer Branding \- What Makes Eduversal Schools Attractive:

steps: Cambridge international curriculum with global qualifications recognized worldwide
Professional development through DTP (Development of Teaching Proficiency) program and Cambridge training
Supportive network of partner schools with opportunities for collaboration and career progression
Quality management systems (EduNav, Appraisal Program, EASE) that provide structure and feedback
Cultural immersion in diverse Indonesian communities with boarding and Islamic school experiences
Competitive compensation and benefits including health insurance, housing support, and visa assistance
Pathway to permanent residency for long-term expatriate staff (KITAP after several years)
Work-life balance with clear term schedules and holiday periods
Bachelor's degree (minimum) in relevant subject or education
Teaching qualification: PGCE (UK), B.Ed., QTS, teaching license, or internationally recognized equivalent
Minimum 2 years teaching experience for standard classroom teacher roles
Minimum 5 years experience for Subject Leader/Head of Department positions
Police clearance from country of origin (required before contract offer)
Physical and mental health fitness (pre-employment medical check)
content.title: Required Qualifications

steps: Master's degree in subject or education
Cambridge International Education training or previous experience teaching Cambridge curriculum
Experience with diverse learners, particularly EAL students
International school teaching experience
Commitment to professional growth and continuous improvement
Science teachers: practical laboratory teaching experience, health & safety certification
Languages: native/near-native proficiency, knowledge of language acquisition methodologies
Mathematics: experience with different international curricula helpful for adapting to Cambridge standards
Humanities: strong content knowledge and research skills to guide extended essays and independent projects

content.text: Indonesian National Subject Teachers: MINIMUM REQUIREMENTS:

steps: S1 (Bachelor's) degree in relevant subject (Pendidikan Pancasila dan Kewarganegaraan, Pendidikan Agama Islam, Bahasa Indonesia)
Sertifikat Pendidik (teaching certification) OR actively working toward certification
Fluency in Bahasa Indonesia (native or near-native)
Understanding of Indonesian national curriculum and assessment frameworks
SKCK (Surat Keterangan Catatan Kepolisian) \- Indonesian police clearance
Commitment to integrating Profil Pelajar Pancasila (P8) competencies
S2 (Master's) degree in subject or education
Experience in bilingual or international school settings
Ability to communicate in English (for collaboration with international colleagues)
Training in active learning pedagogies aligned with Kurikulum Merdeka

content.text: Specialist Roles (detailed in qualification matrix Section 12.2):

steps: Counselors: Psychology/Counseling degree, certification, experience with children/adolescents
EAL Coordinators: TESOL/TEFL/CELTA, knowledge of CEFR framework
SEN Coordinators: Special education qualification, experience with IEPs and Cambridge access arrangements
Librarians: Library Science degree/certificate preferred, digital literacy
Lab Technicians: Technical diploma in relevant field, health & safety knowledge

content.text: Experience Requirements by Role: ![][image2] Safer Recruitment (aligned with Section 9: Safeguarding) CRITICAL PRINCIPLE: All recruitment procedures prioritize student safety. Safer recruitment is a safeguarding responsibility, not merely an HR process. Background Checks and Police Clearance: Indonesian Citizens:

steps: SKCK (Surat Keterangan Catatan Kepolisian) from local police
Valid for 6 months; must be recent (within 3 months of employment start)
Check for criminal record, particularly offenses against children
Police clearance certificate from country of origin or most recent country of residence
Must be authenticated/apostilled if from non-Hague Convention countries
Valid for 6-12 months depending on issuing country
Indonesian SKCK also required after arrival if staying long-term
Police checks show convictions but not all concerns; always conduct thorough reference checks
If gaps in employment history (\>3 months), require explanation
Any disclosed convictions or concerns trigger safeguarding review before hiring decision
Request references from the two most recent employers, including the current or most recent employer.
References must be from direct supervisors, not generic HR letters
Phone or video call verification of written references (to confirm authenticity)
Dates of employment and job title confirmation
Quality of teaching/professional performance
Relationships with students: professional boundaries, appropriate interactions?
Any concerns about candidate's suitability to work with children?
Any disciplinary actions related to safeguarding or professional conduct?
Reason for leaving previous position
Would you re-hire this candidate? Any reservations?

content.text: CRITICAL: "Are you aware of any reason this person should not work with children?" (asked directly) Interview Protocols: Panel Interviews:

steps: Panel of 3: Principal, Subject Leader/HOD, HR representative or GM
Structured interview with predetermined questions (ensures fairness and consistency)
Scenarios/questions probe safeguarding awareness and professional boundaries
"Describe a time when you had concerns about a student's wellbeing. What did you do?"
"How do you maintain professional boundaries with students while building positive relationships?"
"A student asks to connect with you on social media. How do you respond?"
"You suspect a colleague is behaving inappropriately with students. What steps do you take?"
Candidates teach a sample lesson (15-30 minutes) or are observed during a trial day
Assess pedagogical skills, classroom management, rapport with students, subject knowledge
Observe body language, tone, and interactions for appropriateness
Request original or certified copies of degrees, teaching certificates, transcripts
Verify authenticity with issuing institutions if documents seem questionable
For overseas qualifications, check equivalency through Indonesian DIKTI or international credential evaluation services
Verify Cambridge training certificates with Cambridge International Education (if claimed)
All staff undergo medical examination before employment
Screening for communicable diseases (TB, Hepatitis B as per Indonesian regulations)
Assessment of physical and mental fitness for teaching duties
Confidential; results shared only with HR and Principal (not specific diagnoses, only fitness determination)
All new staff: 3-6 month probationary period (Indonesian labor law allows up to 3 months for PKWT (Perjanjian Kerja Waktu Tertentu) contracts)
During probation: close monitoring, regular feedback, observations, support
Performance must meet satisfactory standards to confirm permanent employment
Probation can be extended OR employment terminated with notice if serious concerns arise
Document all performance discussions during probation period
HR screens applications against minimum qualifications
Shortlist candidates who meet requirements and demonstrate strong alignment with school values
Shortlisting criteria: qualifications, experience, clarity of application, alignment with job description
content.title: Selection Process (Step-by-Step)

steps: Video interview (Zoom/Google Meet) for international candidates or in-person for local
30-45 minutes; panel of 2-3 (Principal, HR, and/or Subject Leader)
Assess communication skills, motivation, cultural fit, safeguarding awareness
Preliminary discussion of expectations, compensation, visa process (for expatriates)

content.text: STAGE 3: Teaching Demonstration or Lesson Observation (if initial interview successful) For International Candidates (remote):

steps: Record a 15-20 minute teaching video (specific topic assigned by school)
Submit lesson plan and reflection on teaching choices
Panel reviews video, evaluates pedagogy, engagement strategies, content knowledge

content.text: For Local Candidates or Finalists Visiting School:

steps: Teach an actual class (with school students) for 30-40 minutes
Subject Leader and Principal observe; use observation rubric
Debrief with candidate after lesson to discuss choices and reflections

content.text: STAGE 4: Panel Interview (final stage before offer)

steps: In-person or video interview (60-90 minutes)
Comprehensive assessment: teaching philosophy, subject knowledge, safeguarding, professional scenarios
Discussion of school expectations, contract terms, timeline
Candidate asks questions; ensure transparency about school context (EAL, boarding, Islamic ethos)
Conduct thorough reference checks BEFORE making offer (not after)
Phone/video calls with referees; written references supplemented by conversation
Follow up on any concerns or red flags

content.text: STAGE 6: Offer Letter and Contract Negotiation

steps: Verbal offer followed by formal written offer letter within 24-48 hours
Offer letter includes: position, salary, benefits, start date, conditions (background check, medical)
Negotiate timeline for acceptance (typically 1-2 weeks for candidates to consider)
Once accepted, prepare formal employment contract (Section 12.4)
Background check verification, medical examination, visa application (for expatriates)
Completion of onboarding paperwork (tax forms, BPJS enrollment, emergency contacts)
Arrange housing (for expatriates requiring school-provided accommodation)

content.text: Contract Types (per Indonesian Labor Law UU No. 13/2003): 1. PKWT (Perjanjian Kerja Waktu Tertentu) \- Fixed-Term Contract:

steps: Used for: new teachers (probationary period), short-term replacements, project-based roles
Duration: maximum 2 years for initial contract; renewable once for maximum 1 year
After 3 years total, must convert to PKWTT or end employment
Termination: specific end date; mutual agreement if terminated early
No severance pay required if contract ends naturally (but school may provide transition payment)
content.title: Employment Contracts

content.text: 2. PKWTT (Perjanjian Kerja Waktu Tidak Tertentu) \- Permanent Contract:

steps: Used for: long-term teachers and staff after successful probation/completion of PKWT cycle
Duration: indefinite; continues until resignation, retirement, or termination with cause
Termination: requires valid reasons, due process, and severance pay per labor law
More job security; suitable for stable, experienced staff
International teachers: 1-2 year PKWT contracts (renewable) due to mobility patterns
Indonesian national teachers: PKWT initially, converting to PKWTT after 2-3 years of strong performance
Leadership and support staff: PKWTT for stability
Standard contracts run July-June (aligned with academic year)
Specify exact start and end dates
Notice periods for non-renewal must be given at least 90 days before contract end
Mid-year contracts: prorated for partial year, with option to extend

content.text: Salary and Benefits (Typical Package Components): Basic Salary:

items: Competitive with local international school market
Based on qualifications, experience, and role complexity
Paid in IDR (Indonesian Rupiah) monthly via bank transfer
Subject to Indonesian tax withholding (PPh 21)
Monthly stipend OR school-provided accommodation
Typically 20-30% of basic salary depending on location
May include utilities or be separate
Monthly stipend for commuting (local staff)
School may provide bus/shuttle service
BPJS Kesehatan (Indonesian national health insurance) \- MANDATORY by law
School typically provides additional private health insurance for better coverage
Coverage for employee and dependents (spouse, children)
School covers KITAS (limited stay permit) application and renewal costs
Work permit (IMTA \- Izin Mempekerjakan Tenaga Kerja Asing) processed by school
See Section 2 for detailed KITAS procedures
Participation in DTP (Development of Teaching Proficiency) program
Cambridge training workshops and online modules
Budget allocation for additional PD (conferences, courses)
THR (Tunjangan Hari Raya) \- mandatory religious holiday bonus (1 month salary) for Eid al-Fitr
Performance bonus (optional; based on appraisal results and school financial performance)
BPJS Ketenagakerjaan (employment insurance including old-age savings) \- MANDATORY
Optional private pension scheme (if available)
12-15 days paid leave per year (beyond school holiday periods)
Sick leave: typically 12 days per year (with medical certificate beyond 2 days)
Maternity leave: 3 months (per Indonesian law)
Compassionate leave: case-by-case for family emergencies
One-time payment or reimbursement for moving costs
Initial accommodation arrangement support
End-of-contract repatriation flight (for expatriates returning home)

content.text: Work Hours and Leave Entitlements: Standard Work Week: Monday-Friday, typically 7:00 AM \- 4:00 PM (flexible based on school schedule) Teaching Load:

steps: Primary teachers: 20-24 contact hours per week \+ planning, assessment, duties
Secondary teachers: 18-22 contact hours \+ planning, assessment, pastoral duties
Subject Leaders: reduced teaching load (12-16 hours) for leadership responsibilities

content.text: Additional Duties: homeroom responsibilities, co-curricular activities, parent meetings, professional development Public Holidays: Indonesian national holidays observed (10-15 per year) School Holiday Periods: Term breaks as per academic calendar; teachers generally not required on campus during student holidays (except for planning weeks at start of year/term) Termination Clauses and Notice Periods: Resignation (by employee):

items: Notice: minimum 30 days for mid-year; 90 days if at end of academic year preferred
Must complete handover procedures (Section 12.12)
Penalty may apply for insufficient notice (loss of benefits, negative reference)
School must notify at least 90 days before contract end date
No severance required (contract naturally expires)
Possibility of renewal discussed in Term 2-3
Grounds: serious misconduct, safeguarding breach, gross negligence, prolonged poor performance despite support
Requires documentation, investigation, due process (warnings, improvement plans)
Severance pay may be reduced or waived for serious misconduct (per labor law)
Rare; typically used for redundancy, restructuring
Requires severance payment per UU No. 13/2003 formula (based on years of service)
Example: 1 year service \= 1 month salary; 3 years \= 2 months; 5+ years \= 3 months \+ additional compensation

content.text: Compliance with Indonesian Labor Law (UU No. 13/2003):

steps: All contracts must be in Bahasa Indonesia (English translations acceptable as supplement)
Specify: job title, duties, salary, benefits, work location, contract duration/type
Include: grounds for termination, notice periods, dispute resolution process
Register contracts with local Manpower Office (Dinas Tenaga Kerja) within 7 days
Maintain records for labor inspections

content.text: IMTA (Izin Mempekerjakan Tenaga Kerja Asing) \- Work Permit:

steps: Required BEFORE expatriate can work legally in Indonesia
Applied for by employer (school/Yayasan) through online OSS system (Online Single Submission)
Documents needed: school business license, expatriate's passport, CV, diploma, job description, justification letter
Processing time: 2-4 weeks
Validity: typically 1-2 years, renewable
Annual quota limitations may apply (school can only hire specified number of foreign workers)
content.title: KITAS/KITAP for Expatriate Teachers

content.text: KITAS (Kartu Izin Tinggal Terbatas) \- Limited Stay Permit: Application Process (for new expatriate teachers):

steps: School obtains IMTA approval
Expatriate applies for work visa (Visa 312) at Indonesian embassy/consulate in home country
Expatriate enters Indonesia on Visa 312
Within 7 days of arrival: apply for KITAS at Immigration Office
KITAS issued within 2-4 weeks (initially 1 year validity)
Obtain MERP (Multiple Exit-Reentry Permit) to allow leaving/reentering Indonesia without losing KITAS
KITAS renewed yearly (up to 5 consecutive years)
Documents: passport, IMTA, employment contract, tax ID (NPWP), proof of address
Processing time: 2-3 weeks
School HR typically coordinates renewal process
KITAP (Kartu Izin Tinggal Tetap) \- Permanent Stay Permit
For expatriates committed to long-term residence in Indonesia
Allows work without annual KITAS renewals
Requires continuous KITAS for 5 years, Indonesian language proficiency, recommendation letters
School is legal sponsor (penjamin) for expatriate's KITAS
School responsible for: processing fees, document preparation, liaison with immigration
Expatriate must inform school of passport renewal, address changes, marital status changes
If employment ends: KITAS must be canceled; expatriate has grace period to leave Indonesia or find new sponsor
Expatriate income subject to Indonesian income tax (PPh 21)
Progressive rates: 5% \- 30% depending on income level
School withholds tax from salary monthly and remits to tax office
Expatriate must obtain NPWP (tax identification number) within first months of employment
Annual tax filing required (March deadline for previous year)
If employed \<183 days per year: different tax treatment (non-resident)

content.text: Detailed KITAS procedures: See Section 2: Indonesian Compliance and Regulatory Requirements.

steps: School profile, mission, and organizational chart
Academic calendar, key dates, and school policies (student handbook, staff handbook)
Living in Indonesia guide: culture, customs, language basics, cost of living, healthcare, transportation
Visa process instructions with timeline and required documents
Housing options and neighborhood descriptions (if school does not provide accommodation)
Checklist of items to bring vs. items available locally
content.title: Pre-Arrival Support (for expatriate teachers)

steps: School coordinates short-term accommodation for first 1-2 weeks (hotel or temporary apartment)
Assist in finding long-term rental: arrange viewings, accompany to inspections, negotiate lease
Explain Indonesian rental norms: deposits (typically 1-3 months), annual lease, utility payments, furnishing
Provide sample lease agreement in Bahasa Indonesia with English translation
Airport pickup arranged by school
Orientation to local area: grocery stores, markets, pharmacies, hospitals, banks, post office, places of worship
Assist with: opening bank account (required for salary), purchasing SIM card, registering address with local RT/RW
Connect with other expatriate teachers for community support
Introduction to local transport options (ojek, Grab/Gojek apps, school shuttle)
Basic Bahasa Indonesia lessons (school may provide introductory course)
Assistance enrolling spouse and children in school (if applicable)
Information on dependent visas (KITAS for spouse and children)
Connection to expatriate family groups and community resources

content.text: Welcome Meeting with Principal and HR (Duration: 1-2 hours):

steps: Personal welcome and introduction to leadership team
Overview of school structure, governance (Yayasan, GM, Principal, SLT)
Discussion of role, expectations, and probationary period goals
Review of employment contract and benefits (if not yet fully clarified)
Explanation of payroll process, leave requests, and HR systems
content.title: First Day Onboarding

steps: Walkthrough of campus: classrooms, staff room, library, science labs, sports facilities, canteen, prayer room (if Islamic school)
Introduction to key facilities: photocopier, printer, laminator, resource storage
Safety features: fire exits, first aid kits, emergency assembly points, safeguarding posters
Access arrangements: keys, access cards, parking permits
Issue laptop/tablet (if school-provided) or confirm BYOD policy
Create school email account (Google Workspace or Microsoft 365)
Access to EduNav platform: training on dashboards, KPI tracking, document repository, communication tools
Passwords and login credentials for: learning management system (LMS), student information system (SIS), attendance system
Acceptable use policy for technology (signed)

content.text: Key Policies Briefing (covered on Day 1 or within first week): Safeguarding and Child Protection Policy:

items: DSL (Designated Safeguarding Lead) introduction
Reporting procedures for concerns, low-level concerns, disclosures
Professional boundaries and code of conduct with students
Online safety and digital citizenship expectations
Mandatory safeguarding training scheduled (detailed in Section 12.5)

content.text: Critical: ALL staff sign acknowledgment of safeguarding policy (see Section 9) Staff Code of Conduct and Professional Standards:

items: Professional appearance and dress code
Punctuality and attendance expectations
Respectful communication with students, parents, and colleagues
Confidentiality and data protection
Use of social media and personal devices
How to request leave (advance notice, approval process)
Sick leave protocol: notify Principal/HR by 6:00 AM, provide medical certificate if \>2 days
Substitute teacher arrangements

content.text: Duration: One Full Academic Year All new teachers (both newly qualified and experienced teachers new to the school) participate in a structured induction programme throughout their first year. This comprehensive support ensures smooth integration into Eduversal Partner Schools' culture, curriculum, and quality standards. Structured Support Schedule: Term 1 (First 8-10 weeks): Weekly check-ins (30 minutes) with mentor and/or Subject Leader Term 2 (Weeks 11-25): Fortnightly check-ins (30 minutes) as confidence builds Term 3 (Weeks 26-40): Monthly check-ins (focus on end-of-year reflection and planning) Mentor Assignment:

steps: Every new teacher assigned an experienced mentor (typically 5+ years teaching experience, strong appraisal record)
Mentor provides: guidance, encouragement, practical advice, classroom management strategies, emotional support
Mentor NOT involved in formal appraisal (to maintain trust and openness)
Mentors receive training and modest stipend/recognition for this leadership role
content.title: Induction Framework

steps: Weeks 2-4: Mentor observes new teacher (informal, developmental focus)
Weeks 5-8: New teacher observes mentor and peer teachers (learn effective practices)
Weeks 10-12: Subject Leader conducts formal observation with structured feedback
Term 2: Mid-year observation by Principal or Subject Leader (formative; linked to probation review)
Term 3: Final observation before end of probation period (summative)

content.text: Alignment with DTP (Development of Teaching Proficiency):

steps: Induction is integrated with Eduversal's DTP programme
New teachers participate in DTP workshops: classroom management, lesson planning, assessment design, Cambridge curriculum
Access to online modules and resources through Educamp platform
Tracked in EduNav: completion of induction milestones logged as evidence for appraisal and professional growth

content.text: The induction programme comprises eight core modules delivered through a combination of workshops, online learning, shadowing, and hands-on practice. Each module includes specific learning objectives, resources, and evidence of completion. MODULE 1: School Culture and Mission (Week 1) Learning Objectives:

steps: Understand Eduversal's mission, vision, and values
Learn the six Student Learning Outcomes (SLOs) and how they guide teaching

Using assessment data to inform next steps in teaching

content.text: MODULE 4: Safeguarding and Child Protection (Week 2, Ongoing) Note: This comprehensive module is for teaching staff. A mandatory, role-appropriate version of safeguarding training, focused on core principles and reporting procedures, must be completed by all new employees (including non-teaching and support staff) within their first week of employment, as detailed in Section 9. Learning Objectives:

steps: Understand the school's safeguarding policy and legal responsibilities
Recognize signs of abuse, neglect, and wellbeing concerns in students
Know reporting procedures and low-level concerns process
Maintain professional boundaries and online safety practices
Understand safer recruitment principles (relevant for future involvement in hiring)

items: Overview of safeguarding principles: child protection is everyone's responsibility
Types of abuse: physical, emotional, sexual, neglect; also peer-on-peer abuse, bullying, online exploitation
Recognizing warning signs: behavioral changes, unexplained injuries, withdrawal, academic decline
School's safeguarding policy: read in full; key points highlighted
DSL (Designated Safeguarding Lead) introduction: who they are, how to contact, when to report
Reporting procedures: record concerns on safeguarding form (on EduNav or paper), submit to DSL promptly
Low-level concerns: reporting behaviors that don't meet abuse threshold but raise questions about adult conduct
Confidentiality and information sharing: when and how to share safeguarding information appropriately
Appropriate interactions with students: friendly but professional, avoid favorites, no physical contact except necessary (e.g., first aid, breaking up fights)
One-to-one situations: keep door open, inform colleague, avoid isolated spaces
Social media and communication: no personal social media contact with current students, use school email/platforms only, no private phone numbers shared
Gift-giving and accepting gifts: small tokens acceptable (class gifts), avoid expensive or personal gifts
Transporting students: avoid if possible; if necessary, have another adult present or inform Principal
Teaching students safe internet use: privacy settings, stranger danger online, cyberbullying, digital footprint
Monitoring online platforms used in learning: Google Classroom, Zoom, LMS (ensure appropriate content and interactions)
Reporting online safety concerns: inappropriate content, students sharing personal information, cyberbullying

content.text: Safer Recruitment (if involved in hiring in future):

items: Importance of background checks, reference verification, safeguarding interview questions
Red flags in recruitment: gaps in employment, vague references, reluctance to discuss safeguarding
Your role: observe new colleagues, report concerns about conduct, support induction process

See Section 9: Safeguarding and Child Protection for full policy details and procedures. MODULE 5: EAL and Inclusion (Weeks 4-6) Learning Objectives:

steps: Understand the needs of English as Additional Language (EAL) learners (40-70% of students in Eduversal schools)

content.text: Content: Week 4: Understanding EAL Learners and CEFR Framework:

items: English language proficiency levels: A1 (beginner) to C2 (proficient); most Eduversal students range A2-B2
CEFR can-do statements: what students can understand, speak, read, write at each level
Challenges EAL learners face: academic language vs. conversational language (BICS vs. CALP), content complexity, cultural references, processing time
Assets EAL learners bring: multilingualism, diverse perspectives, resilience
Vocabulary pre-teaching: introduce key terms before lesson, use visuals, provide glossaries
Sentence frames and paragraph structures: "The main idea is... because..." "In conclusion, we can see that..."
Visual supports: diagrams, videos, graphic organizers, mind maps
Simplified language: break down complex sentences, avoid idioms, speak clearly and slightly slower
Collaborative learning: pair EAL learners with supportive peers, use think-pair-share strategies
Extended time for tasks: allow EAL learners more processing and writing time
Multilingual resources: dual-language dictionaries, translated instructions (when appropriate)

content.text: Week 5: MTSS (Multi-Tiered System of Support) Framework:

items: Tier 1 (Universal): High-quality, differentiated instruction for all students in mainstream classroom
Tier 2 (Targeted): Small group intervention for students needing additional support (EAL groups, reading support, math intervention)
Tier 3 (Intensive): Individualized support for students with significant needs (SEN, severe learning difficulties)
Progress monitoring: regular assessments (EASE, formative checks) to identify students needing Tier 2/3 support
Collaboration with EAL Coordinator and Learning Support team

content.text: Week 6: SEN Identification and IEP Implementation:

items: Common special educational needs: dyslexia, ADHD, autism spectrum, dyscalculia, processing disorders, physical disabilities
Referral process: if you notice persistent learning difficulties, consult SEN Coordinator for screening/assessment
IEPs (Individualized Education Plans): tailored goals, accommodations, teaching strategies for SEN students
Your role: implement IEP accommodations (e.g., extra time, separate room, use of laptop, reduced workload)
Communication with SEN Coordinator and parents: regular updates on progress
Students with documented SEN may qualify for accommodations: extra time (typically 25%), separate room, reader, scribe, use of laptop
Application process: SEN Coordinator applies to Cambridge (with evidence); must be done months before exam
Practice accommodations: students must use access arrangements in classroom assessments before exam (to demonstrate normal practice)

steps: Observe an EAL support lesson or co-taught class (if available)
Review sample IEPs and practice planning accommodations for a hypothetical SEN student
Develop a differentiated lesson plan with specific scaffolds for EAL learners (submit to mentor for feedback)

content.text: See Section 8: Inclusion, EAL, and Learning Support for comprehensive guidance on supporting diverse learners. MODULE 6: Teaching and Learning Pedagogy (Ongoing \- First Term) Learning Objectives:

steps: Master effective lesson planning and delivery aligned with Cambridge pedagogy
Develop a repertoire of questioning techniques to promote higher-order thinking
Implement active learning strategies that engage all students
Establish effective classroom management and positive behavior strategies
Provide constructive, timely, and actionable feedback to students
Use technology purposefully to enhance learning

content.text: Content Covered Throughout Term 1 (Workshops, Observations, Practice): Lesson Planning and Delivery:

items: Lesson structure: starter/hook (engage), learning objectives (clarity), main activity (explore), plenary/closure (reflect)
Clear learning objectives: SMART (Specific, Measurable, Achievable, Relevant, Time-bound); shared with students
Differentiation: planning for diverse learners (extension activities for advanced, scaffolds for struggling)
Pacing: balancing teacher talk, student work, collaborative activities; avoiding information overload
Transitions: smooth movement between activities; minimize downtime
Open vs. closed questions: use open-ended questions to stimulate thinking ("Why?" "How?" "What if?")
Bloom's Taxonomy: moving from recall (remember) to higher-order thinking (analyze, evaluate, create)
Wait time: pause after asking question (3-5 seconds) to allow thinking
Cold calling: randomly select students to answer (not just volunteers) to keep all engaged
Think-pair-share: students think individually, discuss with partner, then share with class
Socratic seminars: student-led discussions guided by teacher questions
Hands-on activities: experiments, projects, manipulatives (especially in Science, Math, Design)
Establishing routines: consistent entry/exit procedures, materials management, signal for attention
Clear expectations: co-create classroom rules with students (3-5 key rules), post visibly, reference regularly
Positive reinforcement: praise specific behaviors ("I like how you're listening respectfully"), reward systems
Non-verbal cues: eye contact, proximity, gestures to redirect behavior without disrupting lesson
De-escalation techniques: calm voice, private conversations, choices ("You can work here or at the back table")
Consequences: logical, consistent, fair; progressive (reminder → warning → time out → referral to leadership)
Restorative practices: repairing relationships after conflict, reflection on actions
Refer to school behavior policy for escalation procedures (see Section 7: Student Behavior and Discipline)
Principles: constructive (focus on improvement, not just errors), timely (return work within 1-2 weeks), actionable (specific steps to improve)
Success criteria: share with students before assessment so they know expectations
WWW/EBI (What Went Well / Even Better If): positive feedback \+ improvement suggestions
Marking codes: use school's agreed symbols (e.g., ✓ for correct, circles for errors, "sp" for spelling)
Peer and self-assessment: students evaluate own/each other's work using rubrics (builds metacognition)
Verbal feedback: sometimes more effective than written; record in grade book ("VF given")
Avoid over-marking: focus on key skills, not correcting every error

content.text: Use of Technology and Learning Management Systems:

items: Learning Management System (Google Classroom, Microsoft Teams, or school's LMS): post assignments, resources, announcements
Interactive tools: Kahoot, Quizlet, Padlet, Nearpod for engagement and formative assessment
Video resources: YouTube, TED-Ed, Cambridge Digital Resources (ensure content is age-appropriate and vetted)
Productivity tools: Google Docs for collaborative writing, spreadsheets for data analysis, presentations
Digital citizenship: teach students responsible technology use, citation of sources, avoiding plagiarism
Balance: technology should enhance learning, not replace quality teaching; avoid over-reliance on screens

steps: Observe 3-5 experienced teachers' lessons (different subjects/year levels) with focus on specific pedagogy aspects (e.g., one observation on questioning, one on classroom management)
Develop and teach demonstration lesson with mentor observation and feedback (mid-Term 1)

Reflect on teaching practice weekly in journal: What went well? What would you change? How did students respond?
Lesson plans with increasing sophistication over Term 1

Feedback from mentor observations (both informal and formal)
Reflective journal entries on teaching practice
Completion certificates from DTP pedagogy workshops

content.text: MODULE 7: Assessment and Data Use (Terms 1-2) Learning Objectives:

steps: Understand the school's internal assessment policies and procedures
Learn Cambridge exam requirements for your subject/level (Checkpoint, IGCSE, AS/A Level)
Use assessment data to inform instruction and identify students needing intervention
Track student progress over time and report accurately to parents and school leadership

items: Assessment calendar: when assessments occur (unit tests, end-of-term exams, projects)
Types of assessments: formative (for learning, low-stakes) vs. summative (of learning, high-stakes)
Grading and reporting: school's grading scale, report card timelines, progress reports
Moderation: how teachers ensure consistency in marking (see Section 6: Assessment Policy)
Academic integrity: preventing and addressing plagiarism, collusion, cheating

content.text: Cambridge Exam Requirements (if applicable to your role):

items: Cambridge Checkpoint (end of Lower Secondary): optional diagnostic tests in English, Math, Science; helps track progress
IGCSE (Years 10-11): Cambridge syllabi, coursework requirements (if applicable), exam formats (multiple choice, structured questions, essays)
AS & A Level (Years 12-13): modular exams (AS at end of Year 12, A2 at end of Year 13), practical assessments (for sciences), extended projects
Cambridge exam calendar: entries deadlines (October for May/June exams, April for October/November exams), timetable release, results day
Teacher-assessed coursework and practical skills: submission deadlines, moderation by Cambridge, secure storage of student work
Predicted grades: role in university applications, based on student performance and potential
Collecting data: formative assessments (exit tickets, quizzes), summative assessments (unit tests, projects), EASE results, Cambridge exams
Analyzing data: look for patterns (common misconceptions, topics needing reteaching, students struggling/excelling)
Responding to data: adjust lesson plans, differentiate instruction, provide interventions
Example: "70% of students struggled with simultaneous equations on the quiz → reteach with different approach, provide extra practice"
Use of EduNav dashboards: track class-level and individual student performance over time
Regular assessments: at least one formative check per week (informal), one summative per unit (formal)
Tracking progress: maintain grade book with all assessment scores; identify students not meeting targets
Intervention triggers: student consistently scoring below 50%, significant drop in performance, not completing work
Intervention strategies: extra support sessions, small group reteaching, modified assignments, referral to Learning Support
Communication: inform Subject Leader, counselor, or SEN Coordinator if concerns persist

content.text: Reporting to Parents and School Leadership:

items: Progress reports (mid-term): brief comments on effort, progress, areas for improvement
Term reports: detailed narrative on achievement, behavior, effort; aligned with SLOs
Parent-teacher conferences (PTCs): prepare evidence of student work, data, and specific examples; be constructive and solution-focused
EduNav reporting: upload assessment results and comments to system for transparency and record-keeping

content.text: See Section 6: Assessment Policy for comprehensive guidance on assessment practices, moderation, and reporting. MODULE 8: Pastoral Care and Wellbeing (Ongoing) Learning Objectives:

steps: Understand homeroom teacher responsibilities and expectations
Build positive, supportive relationships with students
Recognize signs of wellbeing concerns (mental health, stress, family issues, bullying)
Refer students to counselors and support services appropriately
Support students' social and emotional development alongside academic growth

content.text: Content: Homeroom Teacher Responsibilities (if applicable to your role):

items: Daily routines: morning check-in (attendance, notices, settling students), afternoon dismissal
Building community: ice-breakers, team-building activities, celebrations (birthdays, achievements)
Monitoring wellbeing: knowing your students' backgrounds, noticing changes in behavior or mood
Communication with parents: regular updates via email or platform, PTC preparation, responding to concerns
Coordinating with subject teachers: gathering feedback on student progress/behavior, addressing concerns collaboratively
Leading homeroom periods: advisory lessons (SEL \- Social and Emotional Learning topics, study skills, goal setting)
Knowing students: learn names quickly, learn about interests/hobbies, greet warmly each day
High expectations \+ high support: believe in every student's potential, provide scaffolding to reach goals
Fairness and consistency: treat all students equitably, apply rules consistently, avoid favoritism
Active listening: give students your full attention, validate feelings ("I can see you're upset about this"), ask open questions
Approachability: students feel comfortable sharing concerns; maintain professional boundaries while being caring
Cultural sensitivity: respect diverse backgrounds, celebrate differences, avoid assumptions
Mental Health Warning Signs: persistent sadness/withdrawal, anxiety (test anxiety, social anxiety), changes in eating/sleeping, talk of self-harm/suicide, panic attacks
Family Issues: parental separation/divorce, financial stress, bereavement, family conflict
Bullying: physical, verbal, social exclusion, cyberbullying; victim may show reluctance to come to school, unexplained injuries, loss of belongings
Academic Stress: pressure to perform, perfectionism, fear of failure, overwhelming workload
Physical Health: frequent headaches/stomachaches, fatigue, unexplained injuries (may also be safeguarding concern)
Substance Use: in older students, signs may include behavioral changes, decline in performance, secretiveness

content.text: Referring to Counselors and Support Services:

items: When to refer: when wellbeing concerns persist or are severe, when student asks for help, when you feel out of your depth
How to refer: speak privately with student, explain that counselor can help, complete referral form (on EduNav or paper), inform counselor and Subject Leader/Principal
Follow-up: check in with student after referral, ask how they're doing (but respect confidentiality of counseling sessions)
Collaboration: counselor may provide strategies for supporting student in class (e.g., allowing breaks, modified workload during crisis)
External services: for severe mental health concerns (suicidal ideation, eating disorders, trauma), school counselor refers to external psychologists/psychiatrists
Explicit SEL instruction: teach skills like empathy, emotional regulation, conflict resolution, decision-making
Modeling: demonstrate respectful communication, patience, resilience when facing challenges
Opportunities for leadership and responsibility: class monitors, peer mentors, student council
Restorative practices: when conflicts arise, facilitate conversations where students take responsibility and repair harm
Celebrating achievements: recognize academic progress, behavior improvements, acts of kindness, contributions to community

steps: Shadow an experienced homeroom teacher for a week to learn daily routines and relationship-building strategies

content.text: See Section 12.8 for connection between appraisal goals and PD planning.
(Outline structure; full content follows detailed pattern) Appraisal Instruments (Eduversal Appraisal Program):

steps: Administrative Domain (20% weight): attendance, punctuality, policy compliance, professionalism
Classroom Observation (60% weight):
Planning and Organization
Classroom Management
Instructional Process
Assessment
Work Inspection (20% weight): lesson plans, student work samples, data records, parent communication
Orientation (Term 1): all teachers briefed on appraisal process, rubrics, expectations
Goal Setting (Term 1): teacher and appraiser co-develop 1-2 professional goals \+ 1 student learning goal
Mid-Year Review (Term 2): progress check, evidence review, adjustments if needed
Formal Observations (Terms 2-3): at least 2 formal observations using Eduversal rubric
Final Appraisal Meeting (Term 3): summative discussion, rating assigned, goals for next year set
Distinguished (4): exceeds expectations, model practice, leadership contributions
Proficient (3): meets expectations, effective teaching, consistent quality
Basic (2): developing practice, some concerns, improvement needed
Unsatisfactory (1): significant concerns, does not meet minimum standards, requires intervention
Appraisal results inform PD priorities
Strong performers identified for leadership roles or mentoring
Teachers on improvement plans receive targeted support via DTP workshops and coaching

content.text: See Teacher Appraisal Handbook and KPI Subject Teacher Guidebook (in project knowledge) for detailed rubrics and processes.

steps: Specific: clearly defined outcome
Measurable: quantifiable or observable
Achievable: realistic given context and support
Relevant: aligned with SIP, student needs, teacher's role

steps: Teacher drafts goals based on self-reflection, appraisal feedback, student data

content.text: (Structure outline; detailed content on supporting staff mental health and preventing burnout) Workload Management:

steps: Reasonable teaching loads (18-24 contact hours per week)

Realistic expectations for extracurricular and additional duties

Avoiding last-minute demands or excessive meetings The Principal will conduct an annual 'workload and communication audit' based on feedback from the staff wellbeing survey to identify and reduce unnecessary burdens (e.g., meeting frequency, reporting deadlines).
Access to employee assistance programs (EAP) or counseling services

Resources: stress management workshops, mindfulness sessions, peer support groups The school commits to providing all full-time staff with access to a confidential Employee Assistance Program (EAP). The contact details for this service will be posted in the staff room and re-communicated to all staff via email at the start of each academic year.
Respect for personal time: minimize emails/communication after hours (except emergencies)
Encourage use of annual leave and sick leave (without guilt)
Flexible working arrangements when possible (remote planning days, adjusted schedules for parents)
Social events and team-building activities to foster community

Redistribute workload if teacher overextended
Encourage self-care: exercise, hobbies, rest, healthy boundaries
Conducted annually (via Google Forms or EduNav)

Demonstrates school values staff voices and commits to continuous improvement
Consistent low appraisal ratings (Basic or Unsatisfactory)
Student complaints or poor student outcomes
Unprofessional conduct (attendance issues, policy violations, safeguarding concerns)
Feedback from observations, work inspections, parent/student surveys

Identify root causes: personal issues, lack of training, unclear expectations?
Offer support: mentoring, reduced responsibilities, access to counseling, targeted PD

content.text: Performance Improvement Plan (PIP): When PIP is Warranted:

steps: Concerns persist despite informal support
Performance gaps are significant and impact student learning or school operations
Clear improvement needed within defined timeline
Specific Concerns: clearly articulated performance gaps (e.g., "Classroom management: students frequently off-task and disruptive; lessons lack structure")
Measurable Improvement Goals: SMART goals for improvement (e.g., "By end of 8 weeks, 80% of observed lessons will show effective classroom management with students engaged and on-task")
Support Provided: coaching, mentoring, targeted PD, additional observations with feedback, resources
Timeline: typically 6-12 weeks with regular progress reviews (weekly or fortnightly)
Progress Monitoring: scheduled observations, work checks, feedback meetings
Documentation: all meetings, support provided, evidence of progress (or lack thereof) documented in writing
PIP developed collaboratively by Principal, Subject Leader, HR
Formal meeting with teacher: The teacher is given at least 48 hours' notice of the meeting's purpose and is informed of their right to be accompanied by a trusted colleague ('plus-one') for support
Regular check-ins: provide constructive feedback, adjust support if needed
Mid-point review: assess progress, decide whether to continue, extend, or conclude PIP
Improvement Achieved: teacher meets goals, demonstrates sustained improvement → return to normal appraisal cycle, continued monitoring
Insufficient Improvement: concerns remain despite support → extend PIP for additional time OR proceed to capability proceedings
Non-Renewal of Contract: for fixed-term contracts (PKWT), school may choose not to renew at end of contract term (requires notice per contract)
Dismissal: for serious misconduct (safeguarding breach, repeated policy violations, gross negligence) or persistent underperformance despite extensive support
All steps documented: concerns raised, support provided, meetings, evidence
Teacher has right to respond, provide their perspective, appeal decisions
Legal and HR consultation before termination (to ensure compliance with UU No. 13/2003)
Severance pay calculated per labor law (if applicable; not required for fixed-term contract non-renewal or termination for serious misconduct)
Performance improvement process is confidential (known only to those directly involved)
Approach is developmental and supportive, not punitive (unless misconduct is severe)
Recognize that not every teacher will succeed in every context; sometimes best outcome is transition to different role or school

content.text: Creating Conditions for Long-Term Commitment: Competitive Compensation and Benefits:

items: Regularly benchmark salaries against comparable schools in Indonesia
Provide comprehensive benefits package (health insurance, housing, BPJS, professional development)
Offer salary increments based on experience, additional qualifications, and strong appraisal performance
THR (religious holiday bonus) and optional performance bonuses

Support for higher education (Master's degrees, Cambridge certifications)
Leadership opportunities: lead PLC, mentor new teachers, coordinate events, serve on committees

Informal: personal thank-you notes from Principal, shout-outs in staff meetings
Peer recognition: encourage teachers to nominate colleagues for recognition
Manageable workload (see Section 12.10)
Respect for personal time and leave

New staff welcomed warmly and integrated into school community
Social events: staff dinners, team-building activities, celebrations

content.text: Purpose of Exit Interviews: When staff resign or contracts are not renewed, conducting exit interviews provides valuable insights for continuous improvement and maintains positive relationships. When to Conduct:

steps: Within last 2-4 weeks of employment
After resignation notice given but before final day
content.title: Exit Interviews

steps: HR representative OR Principal (preferably HR to ensure candid feedback). In cases where a departing staff member's concerns relate directly to the Principal or HR Manager, they will be offered an alternative channel for their exit interview, such as a confidential discussion with the General Manager (GM) or a designated Yayasan representative.
If Principal conducts, ensure setting is non-threatening and confidential
Why have you decided to leave?
What could have been done differently to make you want to stay?
Is there a specific incident or factor that influenced your decision?
What did you enjoy most about working here?
What were the most challenging aspects?
How would you describe the school culture and your relationships with colleagues?
Did you feel supported by school leadership and your direct supervisor?
Were your professional development needs met?
How effective was communication within the school?
Was your workload manageable?
Did you have sufficient time for planning, assessment, and personal wellbeing?
If you were Principal, what changes would you make?
What advice would you give to your successor?
Would you recommend this school to other teachers? Why or why not?
Conduct interview in private, comfortable setting
Explain purpose: to learn and improve, not to persuade them to stay
Assure confidentiality: feedback shared in aggregate, not attributed to individual
Take notes; summarize key themes
Thank departing staff for their service and candid feedback
Aggregate Data: combine feedback from multiple exit interviews over academic year
Identify Patterns: recurring themes (e.g., workload concerns, lack of leadership support, inadequate resources)
Action Plans: develop strategies to address identified issues
Report to Leadership and Yayasan: share findings and improvement plans
Follow-Up: monitor whether changes implemented have improved retention
Departing staff are potential advocates for the school (referrals, positive word-of-mouth)
Stay connected: invite to alumni events, ask for references or support in recruitment
Provide positive references for strong performers (if requested and appropriate)
Celebrate contributions: farewell gathering, card signed by colleagues, recognition of achievements

steps: Contracts: All employment relationships documented in written contracts (PKWT or PKWTT); contracts in Bahasa Indonesia; registered with Manpower Office

content.text: ## Conclusion Effective staff lifecycle management is the foundation of educational excellence at Eduversal Partner Schools. By attracting qualified, mission-aligned educators, providing comprehensive induction and ongoing professional development, conducting fair and developmental appraisals, supporting staff wellbeing, and managing exits professionally, schools create environments where both staff and students thrive. This Section 12 provides Principals, HR teams, and Senior Leadership with the frameworks, processes, and tools needed to build and sustain high-performing educational teams across diverse Indonesian contexts. Consistent implementation of these practices—aligned with Indonesian law, Cambridge standards, and CIS/WASC accreditation requirements—ensures that Eduversal Partner Schools continue to deliver world-class international education that transforms students' lives and futures. Next Steps for Implementation:

steps: Review this section with Senior Leadership Team and HR Manager

Ensure all staff are aware of policies and procedures (staff handbook, orientation sessions)
Train Subject Leaders and mentors in appraisal and coaching techniques

Conduct annual review of staff lifecycle practices; seek feedback from staff; make continuous improvements
